So remember the mistake that most people make with Facebook ads. And this is why people fail with Facebook ads. So Facebook's algorithm is a natural selection machine that's constantly optimizing. And in order to optimize, the algorithm needs variation. And without variation, the algorithm will jam and your ads will stop getting traffic and fail faster than you can imagine. This is why everyone fails with Facebook. And the typical person with Facebook, they have one ad and one image for their ad, and then they target one audience that only has 10,000 people in it. So this is one ad variable being displayed to one audience variable. There is absolutely nothing to be optimized here. It's done. It's at its optimum position immediately. And so this is why people fail with ads. They set the algorithm up to fail. And by doing that, they set themselves up to fail. And this is why people's ads burn out after about two to three days. And when it comes to Facebook ads, variation is key. And what we want to do is we want to have multiple large audiences for testing. So instead of one single small audience, we have multiple large audiences. I know it's pretty much the inverse of what other people teach you how to do. And you also want to have multiple ad angles. So instead of just having one ad angle, you want to have multiple ones coming from different directions. And then within each ad angle, you want to have multiple images per ad angle. And basically what we want to do is load up Facebook's algorithm with multiple audiences, multiple ad angles, and multiple ad images so that it can optimize to find the perfect combinations for the current environment. And it basically looks like this. We've got our multiple ad angles, multiple ad images, and then multiple audiences. So it's doing this at like a million times a second. And it's really shuffling through all of these things, trying to key together the right combination.
